title: Carrot cake, layered
subtitle: A two-layer spiced cake with cream cheese frosting
excerpt: Two 20 cm sponges made with 300 g of grated carrot, sunflower oil, and mixed spice, sandwiched and iced with a tangy cream cheese frosting. The oil keeps the crumb moist for three days. Serves 12.
sourceNotes: Carrot cake in the British tradition is documented from the Second World War as a way to sweeten cakes when sugar was rationed. Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136, and Eliza Acton's Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845) both document carrot-sweetened puddings and confections; the modern layered carrot cake form developed from these roots through the interwar British baking tradition. The cream cheese frosting is an American import that became the British standard from the 1970s onward.

content.text: Carrot cake is an oil sponge: the fat is sunflower oil rather than creamed butter, which means the crumb stays moist for longer and the mixing is simpler. Grated carrot adds sweetness and moisture, and the mixed spice gives the cake its characteristic warmth. The layered version : two sponges sandwiched and iced with cream cheese frosting is the form that has become standard in British baking.

The critical step with the frosting is temperature. Cream cheese frosting made with softened cream cheese and well-beaten icing sugar will be soft; it needs at least 30 minutes in the fridge to firm up before the cake is cut. An
at 300 g of grated carrot per 250 g of flour is well hydrated. You will notice the batter is thicker than a Victoria sandwich batter but more pourable than a scone dough.

items.name: Carrot
items.prepNote: about 4 medium carrots, peeled and coarsely grated

content.text: Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease two 20 cm sandwich tins and line the bases with baking paper. Sift together the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, {{mixed-spice}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} into a large bowl. In a separate bowl, whisk together the {{light-brown-sugar}}, {{eggs}}, and {{sunflower-oil}} until the mixture is smooth and the sugar has dissolved. Pour the wet mixture into the dry ingredients and fold until just combined. Stir in the {{carrot}} grated carrot and the {{walnuts}} chopped walnuts if using. Do not over-mix once the flour goes in; fold in a figure-of-eight motion and stop as soon as no dry flour remains.

Divide the batter evenly between the two tins and smooth the tops with a spatula. Bake for 28 to 32 minutes, until the sponges are a deep golden-brown, have pulled away slightly from the tin sides, and a skewer through the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack. Leave to cool completely, at least 1 hour, before icing.

Beat the {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter with a hand mixer or stand mixer for 2 minutes until pale. Add the {{cream-cheese}} cold cream cheese and the {{vanilla-extract}} vanilla extract and beat until smooth, about 1 minute more. Sift in the {{icing-sugar}} icing sugar in two stages, beating on low to incorporate each addition before increasing the speed. Do not over-beat once all the sugar is in; cream cheese frosting can loosen and become runny if beaten too long after the full sugar is incorporated. Refrigerate the frosting for 15 minutes if it seems soft.

Place one sponge on a serving plate. Spread about a third of the frosting over the top. Set the second sponge on top. Use the remaining frosting to cover the top and sides of the assembled cake, smoothing with an icing spatula. The frosting need not be perfectly smooth on the sides; a slightly textured finish is the carrot cake convention. Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes before slicing.
